title: Queen For A Day

jokes.text: George Michael hiding love letters to his cousin titled 'If You Weren't My Cousin'
jokes.setup: George Michael is introduced as a normal teenager getting ready for school
jokes.punchline: He's hiding romantic letters to his cousin, one explicitly titled 'If You Weren't My Cousin,' which is both specific and incriminating
jokes.explanation: The specific title 'If You Weren't My Cousin' is perfectly constructed — it's confessional, self-aware within the character's logic, and deeply uncomfortable. The narrator describing it as 'particularly incriminating' adds a dry layer. Scores high on character integration because only George Michael's earnest, hopeless romanticism could produce this.

jokes.text: Michael offers George Michael a ride: 'You don't need to be penned up with them. I'll drive you both. We'll take the stairs.'
jokes.setup: Michael offers George Michael a ride to school in the stair car as if it's a normal vehicle
jokes.punchline: 'We'll take the stairs' — treating a comically oversized airport stair car as a routine family car
jokes.explanation: The phrasing 'We'll take the stairs' does the work — it sounds like a practical offer but refers to the absurd vehicle. The joke rewards familiarity with the show's mythology.

jokes.text: George Michael explains kids are still talking about Michael 'taking down that homecoming banner' with the stair car
jokes.setup: George Michael is embarrassed to ride in his dad's vehicle
jokes.punchline: The specific reason — Michael accidentally tore down a school banner with the elevated stair car — is cited as a known social event at the school
jokes.explanation: Works as a cascade — the stair car's ridiculous physical presence in a school parking lot creating social fallout. George Michael being embarrassed by specific documented incidents rather than just general embarrassment adds specificity that elevates it.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'It's not a car.' Beat. 'But- But, no.' When asked if he's embarrassed to be seen in the stair car.
jokes.setup: Michael asks George Michael if he's embarrassed to be seen in 'that car'
jokes.punchline: George Michael reflexively corrects 'It's not a car' — acknowledging the absurdity — then catches himself and tries to be loyal
jokes.explanation: The beat 'It's not a car. But- But, no.' is small but beautifully constructed. George Michael's instinct to be precise overcomes his instinct to be diplomatic, and then he scrambles back. It's pure character comedy delivered in a few words — excellent economy.

jokes.text: Michael's internal monologue: 'I've got to get a new car.'
jokes.setup: Michael just navigated a conversation about his embarrassing stair car while maintaining dignity
jokes.punchline: His private conclusion is the most mundane possible response — not shame, not rage, just 'I've got to get a new car'
jokes.explanation: A functional bridge joke that sets up the episode's A-plot. Works because of Michael's characteristic normalcy amid chaos, but not especially clever.

jokes.text: Maeby's boss: 'Did you get the stack of scripts that I sent you? 'Cause you certainly look well-rested.'
jokes.setup: Maeby has conned her way into a movie studio executive job despite being a teenager
jokes.punchline: Her boss noting she looks 'well-rested' is a cutting passive-aggressive dig implying she hasn't done any work
jokes.explanation: Sharp office sarcasm. Works well as a joke because it's delivered as a compliment while being clearly an indictment. Good economy.

jokes.text: Maeby deflecting her boss's flirtation with 'Yeah. Why don't we ask your wife to come with us?'
jokes.setup: Her boss asks Maeby for a drink, flirting with her — who is actually a teenage girl
jokes.punchline: Maeby shuts him down by invoking his wife — a response far too sophisticated and cutting for her supposed age
jokes.explanation: Maeby's con artist coolness shines here. The joke is that a teenager is navigating corporate sexual politics with more composure than most adults could muster.

jokes.text: Buster explains why he's home: 'everybody's out dancing with their girl and I'm home sitting with Mom.' Michael: 'Mom doesn't wanna go dancing?' Buster: 'I think the age difference is really starting to catch up to us.'
jokes.setup: Buster frames his loneliness in terms of being without a romantic partner
jokes.punchline: Michael assumes Mom is the problem; Buster's response reveals he literally meant his mother IS his girlfriend substitute — and blames their 'age difference'
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional character comedy. The phrase 'the age difference is really starting to catch up to us' applied to his mother is perfectly written — it uses the language of romantic relationships to describe a maternal bond in a way that's both hilarious and deeply uncomfortable. Only Buster can say this. Scores at the top of the episode.

jokes.text: Michael suggests Buster meet women 'who didn't give birth to you'
jokes.setup: Michael tries to encourage Buster to date
jokes.punchline: He has to specify, as a meaningful qualification, that the women should not have given birth to Buster
jokes.explanation: The joke is Michael's resignation that this is a bar that needs to be explicitly set. Economy is excellent — the specificity of 'who didn't give birth to you' does all the work.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'My schedule, however, is as open as my relationship with my wife.'
jokes.setup: Michael declines to go out with Buster due to a busy schedule; Tobias offers to step in
jokes.punchline: Tobias's aside reveals his marriage is 'open' — a joke with multiple layers about his relationship with Lindsay and his obliviousness to its implications
jokes.explanation: A masterclass in Tobias comedy. The simile structure ('as open as my relationship') is elegant, and the double meaning — he intends it as a proud boast, but the audience reads it as confession of a failing marriage — is perfectly calibrated. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Even if it means me taking a chubby, I will suck it up.'
jokes.setup: Tobias offers to be Buster's wingman and 'take one for the team' with an unattractive woman
jokes.punchline: His phrasing — 'taking a chubby' and 'suck it up' in the same breath — is classic Tobias inadvertent double entendre
jokes.explanation: A prime example of the Tobias double-entendre engine. 'Taking a chubby, I will suck it up' is particularly dense — two phrases that work innocently in context and filthily out of it. High character integration because no other character could deliver this.

jokes.text: Michael's reaction beat: 'Boy, that's enough family stuff for today.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has just offered to 'suck it up' while 'taking a chubby' as Buster's wingman
jokes.punchline: Michael's flat, exhausted response is the perfect straight-man button to the absurdity
jokes.explanation: Michael's straight-man exhaustion is a reliable comedic device here. Doesn't break new ground but lands as a clean button on the Tobias joke.

jokes.text: Gob's housing pitch: 'Fifty-two percent of the country is single. That's a market dominated by apartment rentals. Let's take some of that market. I call it Single City.'
jokes.setup: Gob was hired by rival firm Sitwell Enterprises and started with what sounded like a legitimate business pitch
jokes.punchline: The pitch is just: market to single people. He named it 'Single City.' It's breathtakingly undercooked.
jokes.explanation: The joke is how confidently Gob presents the most obvious, underdeveloped idea as genius. The narrator's 'his ideas failed to evolve' sets up the payoff of the pitch's non-evolution perfectly.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'His ideas failed to evolve.' — Cut to Gob pitching 'Swing City'
jokes.setup: Narrator notes that Gob's ideas 'failed to evolve' as he refines Single City into 'Swing City'
jokes.punchline: The 'evolution' is just adding a whirlpool and implying sexual availability. The narrator's clinical descriptor of what is clearly a lateral (or downward) revision
jokes.explanation: The narrator's dry summary of Gob's 'evolution' followed immediately by the whirlpool pitch is a tight irony-undercut structure. The joke is in the gap between 'failed to evolve' and what we see.

jokes.text: Gob's final pitch: 'because sometimes the guys are tapped out. But check your lease, man, because you're livin' in- City.'
jokes.setup: Gob has been building to a third iteration of his 'city' concept
jokes.punchline: He trails off before naming it — either he forgot or it's too inappropriate to say — and replaces it with just '- City'
jokes.explanation: The blank '- City' is the comedic peak of this sequence. The show gives us all the setup — the progressively raunchy pitches — and then the punchline is a void. The audience fills in what's too obscene to name, which is funnier than any actual word would be. Excellent structure and economy. The firing immediately follows, which is perfect timing.

jokes.text: Gob stole Sitwell's dress eyebrows. 'He's not gonna be happy about that.' / 'No, especially when he goes to the opera with two mustaches on his forehead.'
jokes.setup: Sitwell has a disease that makes him completely hairless; Gob was fired and stole something on the way out
jokes.punchline: Gob took his 'dress eyebrows' — and Michael's follow-up image of Sitwell at the opera with 'two mustaches on his forehead' is a specific, absurd visual
jokes.explanation: The concept of 'dress eyebrows' — formal eyebrows for occasions — is wonderfully absurd and specific. Michael's follow-up visual of 'two mustaches on his forehead' is the rare case where the straight man's response is funnier than the setup. Highly quotable and has visual payoff later.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Are you serious? Dad gave you the Camaro? I thought you won that on Card Sharks.' Gob: 'Michael, I was never on Card Sharks. Dad told us not to tell you.'
jokes.setup: Michael is upset that his dad gave Gob a Camaro as a company car but not him
jokes.punchline: Michael believed a false cover story about Gob winning the car on a game show — and the family was in on it together to hide this from Michael specifically
jokes.explanation: The reveal that the whole family conspired to deceive Michael about the Camaro (via a fake Card Sharks story) is both funny and perfectly characterizes the Bluth family dynamic — everyone is casually awful to Michael. The specificity of 'Card Sharks' as the chosen cover story is a great detail.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'At least in prison, we had knife fights, and we had movie night. And once both. Those men did not enjoy Soapdish. I think you have to know that world.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is complaining about being bored hiding in the attic compared to prison
jokes.punchline: Prison had knife fights AND movie nights — and once simultaneously. And the specific movie that caused the knife fight was Soapdish, a 1991 soap opera comedy, which George Sr. defends as a niche taste
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. 'And once both' is a perfect compression — the pivot from 'knife fights' and 'movie night' as separate things to them overlapping is shocking and funny. Then 'Those men did not enjoy Soapdish. I think you have to know that world' is a supremely specific cultural reference used as a deadpan defense of prison violence. The structure is nearly flawless and the Soapdish specificity elevates it far above a generic prison joke.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I'm driving a staircase, for God's sake. It smells like gas. I get hop-ons.'
jokes.setup: Michael confronts his dad about not giving him a company car
jokes.punchline: 'I get hop-ons' — the specific, resigned complaint that random people jump on the stair car
jokes.explanation: 'Hop-ons' is a brilliantly specific coinage — it names the stair car's most ridiculous liability in a single invented word. The list structure ('smells like gas, I get hop-ons') is efficient and escalating.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'Well, of course you're gonna get hop-ons.'
jokes.setup: Michael complains about getting hop-ons on the stair car as if it's an injustice
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s utterly matter-of-fact response — of course that happens, what did you expect — treats it as obvious common sense
jokes.explanation: The straight-faced acceptance of 'of course' in response to a complaint about strangers hopping onto your vehicle is perfectly calibrated. Near-perfect economy.

jokes.text: George Sr.'s guilt trip: 'I gave you company, which, apparently, you don't want to give to me.'
jokes.setup: Michael says his dad never gave him a company car
jokes.punchline: George Sr. pivots on 'company' — he gave Michael 'company' (companionship) rather than a company car — then guilt trips him about it
jokes.explanation: A clean wordplay joke elevated by George Sr.'s manipulative character — he genuinely turns his guilt trip into a pun and expects it to land emotionally. The double meaning on 'company' is well-structured.

jokes.text: Starla's friend commiserating about mothers: 'Moms are such a pain in the ass, huh? It's like "Die already!"'
jokes.setup: Buster is complaining about his mom; a stranger commiserates
jokes.punchline: The commiseration escalates to 'Die already' — a dark extreme that shocks as a casual aside
jokes.explanation: A decent dark joke but the character delivering it is a stranger with no context, which limits the character integration. Works as a setup for Buster's horrified response.

jokes.text: Buster: 'I love my mother. She's one of the two most important people in my life. You know, she and Quincy.'
jokes.setup: Starla's friend says 'die already' about moms; Buster is horrified and corrects
jokes.punchline: Buster's most important people are his mother and... Quincy Jones, apparently
jokes.explanation: The non-sequitur of 'Quincy' (implying Quincy Jones) dropped casually as Buster's second most important person is perfectly Buster — childlike idol worship mixed with mommy issues. Sets up the narrator's disclaimer, which amplifies the absurdity.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'A spokesman for Mr. Jones denies they've ever met, although he could not explain the six signed gold records in Starla's apartment.'
jokes.setup: Buster casually mentions Quincy Jones as one of the two most important people in his life
jokes.punchline: The narrator treats Buster's delusion like a news report — complete with a spokesperson denial and an unexplained detail about gold records
jokes.explanation: The narrator breaking from dry observation to produce a legalistic news-report denial of a throwaway character claim is a brilliant meta move. 'Although he could not explain the six signed gold records' is the perfect detail — it grants the conspiracy theory just enough credibility to be funny, and implies Buster's delusion might have a kernel of truth. Excellent structure.

jokes.text: Starla: 'I could never be with a man who doesn't love his mother.' Buster reacts with romantic surprise.
jokes.setup: Buster has just declared his love for his mother to a stranger in an almost embarrassing way
jokes.punchline: The statement that would normally disqualify a man turns out to be exactly the thing Starla finds attractive — and Buster is stunned it worked
jokes.explanation: The irony is structural: Buster's most disqualifying trait accidentally becomes his best pickup line. The beat works because of Buster's character — his earnest mommy-worship accidentally charms someone.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I'm an ideas man, Michael. I think I proved that with- Mountain.'
jokes.setup: Gob is trying to convince Michael he deserves to be rehired by citing his past successes
jokes.punchline: His best example trails off into just 'Mountain' — presumably a failed or half-conceived idea referenced only by this single vague word
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's sharpest jokes. Gob builds to his credential with confidence, and the entire substance of it is just the word 'Mountain' — unexplained, unreferenced, standing alone. The joke is that Gob considers this self-evidently convincing. Perfect economy, perfect character integration. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I'm gonna prove to you that I'm worthy of rehiring if it takes every ounce of determination that I've got.' Then immediately leaves.
jokes.setup: Gob makes a solemn, passionate declaration that he will prove himself to Michael no matter what
jokes.punchline: He immediately turns and walks out before Michael can respond
jokes.explanation: Classic Gob: maximum bravado, zero follow-through. The joke's structure is perfect — the speech is formally complete and emotionally climactic, and then the punchline is simply that he leaves. Michael's follow-up 'Did you want me to stay and do that?' underlines the absurdity perfectly.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Where are you going?' Gob exits. 'Did you want me to stay and do that?'
jokes.setup: Gob has just left immediately after vowing to prove his worth with every ounce of determination
jokes.punchline: Michael calls after him, then Gob calls back asking if he should have stayed — as if 'doing that' (the passionate speech) is the actual work
jokes.explanation: Button on the previous joke. Works as a second beat — Gob's confusion about whether the speech was the job is the logical extension of his character.

jokes.text: Barry: 'I'll tell you one thing you don't want to do is buy stock in the L.A. Kings.'
jokes.setup: Barry is Michael's inept lawyer, present to give good news about stock being unfrozen
jokes.punchline: Unprompted, Barry warns Michael off the L.A. Kings stock — revealing his investment strategy and incompetence in one aside
jokes.explanation: Barry's aside about the Kings is a reliable character gag — his inability to stay on topic and his terrible financial decisions bleed into the professional advice meeting. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Barry: 'I thought you had four. Oh, you mean a real one.' (regarding Michael wanting a car)
jokes.setup: Michael asks if he can sell stock to buy a car
jokes.punchline: Barry matter-of-factly acknowledges that the stair car is technically a vehicle, then concedes Michael means an actual car
jokes.explanation: Quick, efficient callback joke. Economy is the main asset — 'Oh, you mean a real one' does it in five words.

jokes.text: Michael dictating a letter to investors, Barry suggests perfect wording, then: 'Uh, yeah. Wh-What you just said was perfect. Why do you want me to write it?' Michael: 'Just send it out on your stationery. And do it quickly. I have a car to buy.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks Barry to draft a letter; Barry casually ad-libs the perfect letter verbally
jokes.punchline: Barry is confused why he needs to write it down when he just said it — and Michael's priority is transparently buying a car, not saving the company
jokes.explanation: Sets up a later payoff — none of the family reads the letter. Michael's 'I have a car to buy' is a neat self-indictment. Barry's genuine confusion about the distinction between saying and writing is consistent with his character.

jokes.text: Maeby drops a script on herself while reading secretly. 'Ow! Back to film school-'
jokes.setup: Maeby has snuck upstairs to secretly read scripts she's supposed to have already read
jokes.punchline: She injures herself with the stack of scripts — literally physically harmed by the work she's been avoiding
jokes.explanation: Functional physical comedy. The 'Back to film school' comment from one of the scripts (implied) is a mild extra layer. Not especially inventive but serves the scene.

jokes.text: George Sr. reads the script Maeby dropped. His note: 'Armageddon. That's a bad title.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is desperate for reading material in the attic; he receives a Hollywood script
jokes.punchline: His only literary note on a famous blockbuster title is 'That's a bad title' — delivered with complete authority
jokes.explanation: Extremely economical and dry. George Sr.'s confidence in dismissing 'Armageddon' as a title — a hugely successful film — is classic Bluth delusion. Works both as a critique of his character and as a meta Hollywood joke.

jokes.text: Car salesman: 'Oh, something like this? We have, like, 400 of these.' Michael: 'I don't need anything too popular.' Salesman: 'Oh, they're not popular. We have 400 of them.'
jokes.setup: Michael says he doesn't want something too popular
jokes.punchline: The salesman clarifies that they're not popular — that's why there are 400 unsold — and Michael immediately says 'Sold'
jokes.explanation: Clean misdirection — 'we have 400 of these' reads as popularity, then is recontextualized as the opposite. Michael's instant 'Sold' is the perfect button. Good economy.

jokes.text: Car salesman recognizes Michael: 'Oh, you're the stair car guy. You took down our banner.' Michael: 'Yeah. Sorry. I got a hop-on.'
jokes.setup: Michael is at a car dealership trying to move past the stair car shame
jokes.punchline: The very first place he tries to upgrade is a dealership he embarrassed with the stair car — and his explanation ('I got a hop-on') is matter-of-fact
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback structure — the banner-pulling incident mentioned in the opening scene pays off here at the car dealership. 'I got a hop-on' as the casual explanation for knocking down a dealership banner is perfectly deadpan. Rewards attentive watching.

jokes.text: Salesman lists the car's negatives — no back seat, attracts tickets, insurance costs as much as the last three cars — and Michael fixates on the free windbreaker: 'The windbreaker? Windbreaker comes with it too?' 'I'll take it.'
jokes.setup: The salesman tries to talk Michael OUT of the expensive, impractical car by listing its many flaws
jokes.punchline: Michael ignores all the problems and is won over by a promotional windbreaker — the least significant detail
jokes.explanation: The structure is an inversion: the salesman is actively trying to lose the sale, and Michael counters each red flag with obsession over the windbreaker. The 'I'll take it' after a litany of warnings is a satisfying comedic payoff. Sets up the later thematic callback about sensible vs. flashy choices.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Michael had just indulged himself with an expensive sports car—' [cuts to Michael already at the dealership signing] 'And here again, please.'
jokes.setup: The narrator announces Michael bought an expensive car
jokes.punchline: We cut to Michael still in the process — specifically at the part where he signs yet another page — the mundane reality of a large purchase undercuts the narrator's dramatic framing
jokes.explanation: A small but effective structural joke — the narrator's framing is grander than the reality, which is just paperwork. 'And here again, please' deflates the drama nicely.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'This place is exactly what I had hoped for.' Cut to: it's a women's bar. Buster: 'All ladies.' Tobias: 'Mmm.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has brought Buster to a bar to meet women; he declares it perfect
jokes.punchline: The bar is entirely women, which Tobias declares ideal — and his 'Mmm' of satisfaction is ambiguous in a way consistent with his character
jokes.explanation: Works because of Tobias — his satisfaction at a room full of women to assist Buster feels completely genuine while also reading as exactly the wrong motivation. The 'Mmm' is doing character work without a single explicit joke.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Well, I've heard that this uniform is the best way to meet—' [interrupted by Lucille 2]
jokes.setup: Tobias is wearing some kind of uniform to help Buster meet women
jokes.punchline: He's cut off before completing the sentence — implying the uniform choice is somehow strategic in a Tobias way, left to the audience's imagination
jokes.explanation: A setup with a withheld punchline — the audience is left to imagine what Tobias's uniform theory is. Intriguing but incomplete as a standalone joke.

jokes.text: Lucille 2 grabs Buster and begins spinning. 'And I am rock steady! No more dizzies!' [immediately gets vertigo and falls]
jokes.setup: Lucille 2 has just returned from vertigo treatment and declares herself 'rock steady'
jokes.punchline: She immediately gets vertigo — presumably from the spinning hug she initiated
jokes.explanation: Classic sitcom irony — the character who has just been declared cured immediately demonstrates they're not. The physical payoff is clear from context. 'Rock steady' as her exact declaration makes the fall more ironic. Good structure.

jokes.text: Buster: 'Well, I would hardly call my mother a girl.' (responding to Lucille 2 asking if there's a girl in his life)
jokes.setup: Lucille 2 asks Buster if there's 'a girl in his life'
jokes.punchline: Buster's answer is about his mother — but rather than being ashamed, he corrects the terminology: his mother is not a 'girl,' she's a woman. He takes the correction seriously.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional Buster joke. 'I would hardly call my mother a girl' is devastating — his objection is to the diminutive, not to the fact that his answer to 'is there a girl in your life' is his mother. The character integration is as high as it gets; this could only ever be Buster. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: Lucille 2: 'No, I mean, uh, someone who makes you hear music.' Buster: 'Oh. Well, she mostly likes talk radio.'
jokes.setup: Lucille 2 tries to romantically ask if Buster hears music when he's with someone special
jokes.punchline: Buster interprets 'makes you hear music' literally and reports on his mother's radio preferences
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best exchanges. Lucille 2's romantic idiom crashes into Buster's literal-minded interpretation, and 'she mostly likes talk radio' is the perfect non-answer answer — it gives us too much information while answering the wrong question entirely. The structure of repeated romantic setup/literal deflection in this scene is beautifully executed.

jokes.text: Lucille 2 tries again: 'and also who makes you hear beautiful music.' Beat. Buster: 'Oh. No.'
jokes.setup: Lucille 2 elaborates her romantic question, adding 'beautiful music'
jokes.punchline: A beat of silence followed by Buster's flat 'Oh. No.' — he has finally understood the question is about romance, and his mother fails this test entirely
jokes.explanation: The delayed 'Oh. No.' is perfectly constructed. The pause does work — Buster processes that the question was romantic, reviews his mother against this criterion, and returns a simple negative. The economy here is extraordinary; two syllables complete a joke that required multiple setups. Feels like a room-stopper.

jokes.text: A random bar patron steps in front of Lucille 2 and Buster and begins singing Sinatra. 'Everybody thinks they're Frank Sinatra.'
jokes.setup: Lucille 2 is trying to romantically guide Buster in a quiet moment of connection
jokes.punchline: A stranger spontaneously begins performing Sinatra, and Lucille 2's response is world-weary resignation — 'everybody thinks they're Frank Sinatra'
jokes.explanation: A mild absurdist interruption. Works as a scene break and a character beat for Lucille 2 but isn't especially original. The Sinatra intrusion feels a touch convenient.

jokes.text: Lucille locked Buster on the balcony. Michael: 'What tripped you off? When she locked you out on the balcony again?' Buster: 'That was half my fault. I thought I saw a graham cracker out there.'
jokes.setup: Buster is complaining his mom is controlling; we learn she locked him out on the balcony
jokes.punchline: 'I thought I saw a graham cracker out there' — Buster was lured by a graham cracker, which he accepts as legitimate mitigation of her locking him out
jokes.explanation: Exceptional. The joke has multiple layers: (1) Lucille baits the balcony with snacks to trap her adult son, (2) Buster knows this and still fell for it, (3) he considers this 'half his fault' suggesting this has happened enough times to have assigned blame. 'Graham cracker' as the specific bait is perfect — infantile, specific, and completely believable for Buster. One of the episode's best.

jokes.text: Michael: 'You baited the balcony?' Lucille: 'Prove it.'
jokes.setup: Buster revealed that Lucille lured him onto the balcony with a graham cracker
jokes.punchline: 'Prove it.' — Lucille admits nothing and immediately goes into legal defense mode, treating her son's entrapment as a crime that requires evidence
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quotable moments. 'Prove it' in response to 'You baited the balcony?' is the perfect Lucille response — zero remorse, immediate deflection, treating maternal manipulation as a legal matter. The economy is flawless: two words do everything.

jokes.text: Lucille explains the remodeling: she had Lucille 2's construction crew do her bathroom instead. 'Well, I sat on one of these jets. But I think we can fix it.'
jokes.setup: We learn Lucille redirected Lucille 2's remodeling crew to expand her own bathroom
jokes.punchline: Lucille's nonchalant explanation of why the tub is bigger — she sat on a jet — delivered as an aside with 'but I think we can fix it'
jokes.explanation: The image of Lucille Bluth sitting on a jacuzzi jet and needing it replaced is both vulgar and very specifically Lucille — indulgent, unapologetic, blaming the equipment. 'But I think we can fix it' adds a casual optimism that makes the reveal funnier.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'I moved the wall a couple of inches into her penthouse.'
jokes.setup: Lucille had supposedly let the construction crew into Lucille 2's penthouse; we expect she did something passive-aggressive
jokes.punchline: She literally stole square footage from her neighbor by moving the wall — the most tangible, physical form of pettiness
jokes.explanation: Lucille's revenge being literal property theft — stealing inches of real estate from a neighbor — is wonderfully specific and escalatory. It takes passive-aggressive behavior to a physical extreme that only Lucille could justify to herself.

jokes.text: Lucille sold stock after reading Barry's letter. Michael: 'I sent you that letter. It says not to sell.' Lucille: 'It does? I stopped at "unfrozen."'
jokes.setup: Michael discovers Lucille sold stock after the letter he sent specifically saying not to sell
jokes.punchline: She only read the word 'unfrozen' and stopped — the most relevant word to her immediate desires
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best structural jokes — and a perfect payoff to the letter setup from earlier. 'I stopped at unfrozen' is both hilarious and completely true to Lucille's character: she has the attention span of someone who only processes information that benefits her. The identical revelation will be echoed by other characters, creating a running gag.

jokes.text: Gob: 'What? No. I'm trying to get a job from you, Mikey. Come on.' (re: selling stock)
jokes.setup: Michael asks Gob if he sold his stock; Gob denies it with injured dignity
jokes.punchline: Gob's reason for not selling has nothing to do with loyalty — it's that he needs Michael's goodwill for a job
jokes.explanation: A functional character joke — Gob's loyalty is entirely self-interested. Doesn't land as hard as the other 'didn't read the letter' reveals but adds to the pattern.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'No, no, no. The club. I was dancing with what turned out to be the club's owner, and he was looking to sell.' Gob: 'Oh, he really, really did look like a woman.'
jokes.setup: Tobias announces he bought 'the Queen Mary'; Michael assumes he means the ship
jokes.punchline: It's a club — and the owner 'looked like a woman,' which Gob confirms with emphasis, which adds a layer about what Tobias was doing on the dance floor
jokes.explanation: Gob's confirmation that the male club owner 'really, really did look like a woman' adds a retroactive layer to Tobias's dancing story. The double meaning — was Tobias romantically dancing with the owner? — is left open and is funnier for it.

jokes.text: Tobias defends the Queen Mary club purchase: 'can you believe that the only reason the club is going under is because it's in a terrifying neighborhood?'
jokes.setup: Tobias has spent the family's stock money on a failing nightclub
jokes.punchline: His framing of 'terrifying neighborhood' as a baffling, inexplicable mystery — rather than an obvious business liability — reveals his complete disconnection from reality
jokes.explanation: Tobias presenting 'terrifying neighborhood' as an incomprehensible mystery is a great obliviousness gag. His genuine puzzlement at an obvious business problem is well-characterized.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Did you guys not read the letter?' Narrator: 'None of them had read past the word "unfrozen."'
jokes.setup: Michael is shocked that everyone spent their stock money
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals all of them stopped reading at the same word — 'unfrozen' — as a collective family failure
jokes.explanation: The collectivization of 'I stopped at unfrozen' into a family-wide phenomenon is excellent structure. The narrator delivering the confirmation makes it feel like a judgment from the universe. This is Arrested Development at its best — the first callback pays off in a bigger payoff.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'Hey, Dad, is that your new car in the driveway? I can't believe you got a Corvette!'
jokes.setup: Michael just finished lecturing his family about their selfish spending sprees
jokes.punchline: His son immediately reveals Michael bought an expensive sports car — a Corvette — undermining his entire moral authority
jokes.explanation: Perfect structural payoff — Michael finishes his 'I hope you're all proud of yourselves' speech and is immediately, publicly undone by his own son. The Corvette reveal as a button on the lecture scene is beautifully timed. Exactly the kind of ironic juxtaposition Arrested Development does best.

jokes.text: Michael: 'It's a company car.'
jokes.setup: George Michael has just called out Michael's Corvette in front of everyone
jokes.punchline: Michael's embarrassed defense is identical to the rationalization he used when buying the stair car — 'it's a company car' — which is both weak and hypocritical given his speech
jokes.explanation: Three words, perfect economy. 'It's a company car' after a lecture about selfishness is devastating. The callback to his own rationalizing throughout the episode makes this the button on the entire car subplot. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: Barry: 'That's what they said on AskJeeves.'
jokes.setup: Barry is citing the source for his legal/financial advice about stock control
jokes.punchline: His source is AskJeeves — an early-2000s search engine — not legal expertise or precedent
jokes.explanation: A clean Barry incompetence joke. 'AskJeeves' is a perfectly chosen period-specific reference — it signals not just that he googled it but that he's using a slightly outdated search engine, which is exactly Barry's level. Good economy.

jokes.text: The hostile company is called 'Standpoor' — 'as in the opposite of Sitwell... which is run by a hairless man who could be very upset with you right now.' Michael: 'What would he have to be upset with me about?' [beat] 'You're wearing his eyebrows?'
jokes.setup: The company that bought Bluth stock is called 'Standpoor'; Barry explains the name
jokes.punchline: 'The opposite of Sitwell' — a pun on 'sit well / stand poor'; and Gob is literally wearing Sitwell's stolen eyebrows during this conversation
jokes.explanation: A layered joke: the company name is a pun on Sitwell's name, the villain is established from earlier in the episode, AND Gob is wearing the stolen eyebrows during the reveal. The convergence of the wordplay and the physical callback happening simultaneously is excellent Arrested Development density.

jokes.text: Gob is still wearing Sitwell's stolen eyebrows: 'They make me feel dressier.'
jokes.setup: Michael realizes the majority shareholder might be connected to Sitwell; Barry reveals Sitwell might be upset
jokes.punchline: Gob is literally wearing Sitwell's stolen dress eyebrows on his face right now, and when Michael asks why Sitwell would be upset, Gob gestures to his borrowed eyebrows with 'They make me feel dressier'
jokes.explanation: Great callback payoff. Gob wearing the stolen eyebrows as if it's fashion is perfect visual comedy. 'They make me feel dressier' as a sincere explanation for grand theft eyebrows is deeply Gob. The rewatch bonus comes from knowing the eyebrows are Sitwell's formal set.

jokes.text: Michael's phone call: 'Kind of. To something called Standpoor. Yeah, it's Sitwell.' George Michael on the other end: 'It's Lucille Austero.' Michael: 'Lucille 2?' 'Standpoor, because she can't stand up without falling over.'
jokes.setup: Michael assumes Standpoor is Sitwell; George Sr. corrects him — it's Lucille 2
jokes.punchline: A second pun explanation: Standpoor because Lucille 2 can't stand without vertigo — the name works two ways
jokes.explanation: The pun works on both levels simultaneously — opposite of Sitwell AND can't stand upright — which is the kind of dense layered construction Arrested Development is known for. The reveal subverts the expected answer (Sitwell) and rewards viewers familiar with the vertigo gag.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'I don't know why she'd have any trouble. A piano could stand on those legs.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is commenting on Lucille 2's vertigo/inability to stand
jokes.punchline: Lucille 2's legs are apparently very sturdy — this is both a backhanded compliment and a slam, delivered with Lucille's characteristic venom
jokes.explanation: A wickedly specific Lucille insult — 'a piano could stand on those legs' insults Lucille 2's physicality while ostensibly defending her. Only Lucille Bluth could deliver this without irony while meaning everything ironically.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'I think we're gonna need a new floating thermometer.' (after explaining the Lucille 2 revenge plot)
jokes.setup: Michael and Lucille are in what is implied to be the expanded bathroom/tub
jokes.punchline: Lucille's casual mention of the floating thermometer suggests the tub overhaul is now complete enough to accessorize, and she's treating her theft of square footage as a home improvement project
jokes.explanation: A small visual-adjacent joke — the floating thermometer detail grounds the bathroom setting absurdly. Works as Lucille character comedy but is a minor note.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Give her Buster.' Michael: 'What?' Lucille: 'We need our company back.'
jokes.setup: Michael suggests reasoning with Lucille 2 to get the stock back
jokes.punchline: Lucille's solution is to offer her son as a romantic pawn — matter-of-factly, as if bartering a child is a reasonable business strategy
jokes.explanation: 'Give her Buster' is a quintessentially Lucille line — treating her adult son as a tradeable commodity for business purposes. The flatness of delivery and the immediate justification 'We need our company back' makes it funnier. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: Starla's roommate: 'She's saying good-bye to someone named Q. If she can get past his security system, she's gonna tell him it's over.'
jokes.setup: Michael is looking for Starla; the roommate explains where she is
jokes.punchline: Starla's ex is so famous/important that reaching him requires navigating a security system — implying 'Q' is Quincy Jones, who apparently has security that screens people trying to break up with him
jokes.explanation: The continuation of the Quincy Jones subplot — he has a security system that must be penetrated for a breakup — escalates the absurdity appropriately. The bureaucratic difficulty of breaking up with a celebrity is a solid premise.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Starla being a woman that wasn't 70.'
jokes.setup: The narrator explains why Buster and Starla hit it off — Buster is a 'sweeter, more supportive man than she was used to'
jokes.punchline: And Starla's key advantage is simply: she isn't 70 — i.e., she isn't Lucille
jokes.explanation: The narrator's dry delivery of 'a woman that wasn't 70' as Starla's primary credential is exactly the show's sensibility — understated, precise, and devastating about Buster's situation without being cruel. Economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: Michael's metaphor to Buster: 'You know, you want something sensible, you end up getting pushed into something flashy.' Buster: 'What does that mean?'
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to counsel Buster about his relationship choices using metaphor
jokes.punchline: The metaphor is transparently about Michael's own Corvette purchase — he's literally describing what happened to him at the dealership — and Buster rightly doesn't understand it
jokes.explanation: Michael's advice is autobiographical self-criticism disguised as wisdom for Buster. Buster's genuine confusion ('What does that mean?') punctures the pretense perfectly. Good structural use of the episode's thematic throughline.

jokes.text: Michael to Buster: 'Don't you think you belong in a Lucille?'
jokes.setup: Michael is continuing the car metaphor (sensible vs. flashy), now naming the 'sensible' option
jokes.punchline: 'Lucille' is both a car model and, of course, Buster's mother — Michael accidentally suggests Buster should be with his mom
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes and a showpiece of Arrested Development's layered construction. Michael uses 'Lucille' as a car name in his metaphor, which is also Buster's mother's name, which is also literally what Buster is being pushed back toward. The triple meaning lands simultaneously, and the fact that Michael says it accidentally makes it funnier. Outstanding wordplay and character integration.

jokes.text: George Michael explains he wrote notes on Maeby's scripts. Maeby: 'They were great. I mean, they said so many things I wanted to say, but I didn't know how.' George Michael: 'Don't tell anyone, okay?' 'Never.'
jokes.setup: Maeby thinks someone gave helpful script notes; the audience knows these are love letters that happen to work as script notes
jokes.punchline: George Michael's 'Don't tell anyone' is about the romantic content; Maeby's 'Never' is about protecting her studio job secret — both are keeping the same secret for different reasons
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed dramatic irony payoff. The letters serve double duty — romantic missives that accidentally function as professional script notes — and both characters agree to secrecy for completely different reasons. Rich in layers and rewards rewatch.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'I guess she didn't see the drawings.'
jokes.setup: Maeby loved George Michael's 'notes'; George Michael hopes his secret is safe
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals the letters had drawings — presumably romantic/explicit drawings of Maeby — that she somehow missed
jokes.explanation: The narrator's throwaway line is the episode's darkest and funniest twist on the love letter plot. 'I guess she didn't see the drawings' implies George Michael drew things that would be unambiguously incriminating — the joke is entirely in what's not said. Perfect economy, and the delayed revelation makes the earlier scene much funnier in retrospect.

jokes.text: Tobias confronts the sign painter: 'No, no. It's supposed to say "Tobias's Queen Mary." No, that is not what it says.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has taken ownership of the Queen Mary club and is having it repainted
jokes.punchline: The sign apparently reads something other than 'Tobias's Queen Mary' — what it says is unspecified but clearly wrong/embarrassing
jokes.explanation: A setup joke for the 'Tobias is Queen Mary' sign payoff that comes later. Mildly funny on its own; gains more from context.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Well, I suppose I could just paint over "Mary."' Neighbor: 'Don't bother, man. This is our club now.'
jokes.setup: The sign reads something with 'Mary' in it that Tobias wants to fix
jokes.punchline: Tobias considers just painting over 'Mary' — leaving 'Tobias is Queen' — without recognizing the implication
jokes.explanation: Classic Tobias inadvertency — painting over 'Mary' would leave 'Tobias is Queen,' which he does not recognize as a problem. The narrator later confirms the sign reads 'Tobias is Queen Mary,' which generates the episode's running visual gag. High character integration.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Tobias didn't want to sell his new club, but Lindsay did. Which is why she hired several members of a local stripper agency called the Hot Cops to pose as the roughnecks.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay wants to scare Tobias out of the club he just bought
jokes.punchline: Her plan involves hiring 'Hot Cops' — an all-male stripper agency — to pose as dangerous neighborhood criminals
jokes.explanation: The Hot Cops as neighborhood toughs is a good premise — the joke is that the most threatening people Lindsay could hire are actually strippers. Works as setup for Tobias's theatrical reaction.

jokes.text: Tobias to the Hot Cops: 'I know you know nothing but a life on the street, but I'd like to offer you something that the Queen Mary gave me. The joy of the stage. So maybe you could, uh, start jeté-ing and stop "je-terrorizing" me.'
jokes.setup: Tobias believes the Hot Cops are genuine roughnecks threatening his neighborhood
jokes.punchline: 'Je-terrorizing' — a pun on jeté (ballet term) and terrorizing — is characteristic Tobias theatrical wordplay delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout jokes. 'Je-terrorizing' is a genuinely inventive pun — jeté + terrorizing — delivered by a character who is simultaneously being shaken down by fake thugs while proposing a dance therapy intervention. Only Tobias could coin this word and mean it earnestly. The whole speech is perfectly in character and builds to the punchline beautifully.

jokes.text: Tobias: '...maybe you could start jeté-ing and stop "je-terrorizing" me.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is recruiting the Hot Cops into dance theater by contrasting their street lifestyle with ballet
jokes.punchline: 'Je-terrorizing' — a portmanteau of 'terrorizing' and 'jeté' (the ballet jump) — delivered as a genuine pun Tobias is proud of
jokes.explanation: A standout pun. 'Je-terrorizing' merges a French ballet term with a crime word in a way that is both terrible and brilliant — exactly Tobias's sensibility. The setup (he wants them to jeté, not terrorize) makes the pun structurally earned rather than random. Highly quotable and deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Buster, now convinced that Lucille 2 was the right woman for him, broke up with Starla.' [Buster arrives looking dejected] Starla: 'You okay?' Buster: 'It was for the best anyway. A girl like that deserves to be taken care of, and I'm just a poor soldier.'
jokes.setup: Buster has left Starla for Lucille 2, who is older and wealthier
jokes.punchline: Buster's noble self-sacrifice speech — 'I'm just a poor soldier' — is undercut by the fact that he's leaving a younger, warm woman for a wealthy older one because his mom approves
jokes.explanation: Buster's self-delusion is well-captured here — he casts himself as a tragic figure making a sacrifice while actually doing the opposite of what would be healthy for him. Character comedy that works without being extraordinary.

jokes.text: Michael realizes Buster didn't sell his stock. Buster: 'Your letter said not to, and I would never want to hurt the family.'
jokes.setup: Michael has been discovering that everyone read the letter and ignored the warning
jokes.punchline: Buster — the family member everyone least expects to be responsible — is the only one who actually read and heeded the letter
jokes.explanation: The ironic reveal that Buster — widely treated as the family's least competent member — is the only one who acted responsibly is a well-earned character moment that also deepens the comedy of the earlier 'unfrozen' gag.

jokes.text: Michael tells Buster to go back to Lucille 2: 'I'll take care of Lucille 2 for you. Let her down easy. That right knee could go at any time.'
jokes.setup: Michael is being genuine about helping Buster get back with Lucille 2
jokes.punchline: 'Let her down easy. That right knee could go at any time' — Michael's concern is both physically literal (her knee) and a dark joke about her age/fragility
jokes.explanation: The non-sequitur concern about Lucille 2's knee is delivered deadpan and lands well as an ageist aside from the show's normally upright protagonist. Shows Michael is more Bluth than he'd like to admit.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And at the "Tobias is Queen Mary"...'
jokes.setup: The narrator needs to refer to Tobias's club
jokes.punchline: The club is now officially called 'Tobias is Queen Mary' — the sign mistake has become the establishment's name
jokes.explanation: The narrator treating 'Tobias is Queen Mary' as the venue's proper name is the payoff of the sign gag. It's delivered as matter-of-fact location description, which is the correct comedic choice.

jokes.text: Tobias watches his Hot Cops protégés dance in the club: 'Wow. You really have made some amazing strides in a half an hour.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has spent 30 minutes 'teaching' the Hot Cops (actually trained strippers/dancers) dance moves
jokes.punchline: He's amazed by the progress of professional dancers he thinks are street toughs — his sense of accomplishment is completely unearned
jokes.explanation: Tobias's pedagogical pride in teaching people who were already experts is a reliable comedy structure here. Works as character comedy though not exceptional on its own.

jokes.text: Tobias leads his Hot Cops gang into the street to perform tap dance as gang deterrence. They break into: 'So you think you're tough / Well, we hate rappin' / But don't bust a cap in / 'Cause here's what's happenin' / We're breakin' out some old-fashioned tappin''
jokes.setup: Tobias has convinced the Hot Cops that tap dancing can 'change the streets'
jokes.punchline: They perform an actual tap-dance rap number in front of real gang members — an utterly delusional response to street violence
jokes.explanation: The absurdity of the tap-dance gang intervention is fully committed to and richly deserved after the setup. The rap lyrics ('we hate rappin'... old-fashioned tappin'') are genuinely clever in their anti-rap self-awareness. The physical payoff of Tobias actually doing this is a season highlight.

jokes.text: Tobias's tap rap: 'So you think you're tough / Well, we hate rappin' / But don't bust a cap in / 'Cause here's what's happenin' / We're breakin' out some old-fashioned tappin''
jokes.setup: Tobias's gang confronts street toughs and begins their artistic intervention
jokes.punchline: The rap's lyrics are simultaneously anti-rap ('we hate rappin''), anti-violence ('don't bust a cap in'), and a promotion for tap dancing — delivered as a sincere gang deterrent
jokes.explanation: The internal rhyme scheme ('rappin'/cap in/happenin'/tappin'') is actually technically proficient, which makes the absurd content funnier. 'We hate rappin'' as the opening of a rap is perfect. The AABB rhyme somehow working while making zero strategic sense is a genuine craft achievement. One of the episode's comedic highlights.

jokes.text: 'Oh, I've been shot!' Tobias: 'We miscalculated! Retreat!' 'Quick, switch to Follies!' 'That's not gonna help!'
jokes.setup: The tap dance is not deterring anyone and someone gets shot
jokes.punchline: Tobias's tactical response to gunfire is to switch from tap to 'Follies' — another dance form — which someone correctly identifies as equally unhelpful
jokes.explanation: The escalation from tap dance to gunfire is perfectly paced. 'Quick, switch to Follies!' as a tactical command during a shooting is one of the funniest lines in the episode — Tobias's frame of reference is so thoroughly theatrical that gunshots register as a need for a different dance style. The response 'That's not gonna help!' is the ideal button. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Meanwhile, Michael and Lucille 2 arrived to find the club nearly empty. Although Michael would have fit in perfectly.'
jokes.setup: Michael arrives at the Queen Mary (the gay club named 'Tobias is Queen Mary')
jokes.punchline: The narrator dryly notes Michael 'would have fit in perfectly' — implying the club's clientele is consistent with Michael's appearance or demeanor in some way
jokes.explanation: A classic Arrested Development narrator aside — the information is delivered completely neutrally while implying something that the character would object to. Works well within the show's meta-commentary on its own characters.

jokes.text: Michael confesses to Lucille 2 that he sent Buster to date her to get the company stock back. 'The only reason that he asked you out tonight was because I wanted him to get the stock back from you so that I could regain control of the company. But I would— I'd rather have my brother back. So— I'm sorry.'
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to charm Lucille 2 into returning the stock
jokes.punchline: He abandons the charm offensive and confesses the entire manipulation, making the situation much worse while believing he's being noble
jokes.explanation: Michael's compulsive honesty backfiring is a character throughline. Works as cringe comedy — watching him confess things he shouldn't — but the punchline is delayed by plot resolution, which softens the comedy.

jokes.text: Lucille 2: 'Like the Bluths? I love the Bluths. Why did you buy up all of our company stock?' 'To support you guys.' 'I have always had a standing order to buy up any available Bluth stock.'
jokes.setup: Michael assumes Lucille 2 bought the stock as revenge for the bathroom remodel
jokes.punchline: She bought it out of support and has been doing so all along — the 'villainous' act was actually an act of love, making Michael's entire scheming this episode unnecessary
jokes.explanation: A good structural reversal — the episode's entire conflict was based on a misread. The irony that the Bluths exhausted themselves fighting a supporter is classic Bluth dysfunction. Works more as plot comedy than pure joke.

jokes.text: Lucille 2: 'Actually, there is a condition.' Michael: 'Anything.' Lucille 2: 'Call me a cab.' Michael: 'Ah, because of my driving. Yes.'
jokes.setup: Michael agrees to any condition to get the stock back
jokes.punchline: The condition is just: don't drive her. Michael's Corvette driving triggered her vertigo.
jokes.explanation: A clean callback payoff — the expensive car that Michael justified as a 'company car' literally caused the plot problem it was supposed to solve. Michael's 'yes' of resigned acceptance is the correct button. Good structural economy.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I fixed everything.' Lucille: 'Oh?' Michael: 'With the business. It's all taken care of.' Michael: 'You didn't think I could, but I did.'
jokes.setup: Michael is triumphant about fixing the company situation
jokes.punchline: His pride is immediately about to be undone by Gob
jokes.explanation: Setup for the Gob reveal. Works as dramatic irony but isn't especially funny on its own — the comedy is in the contrast with what follows.

jokes.text: Gob: 'My mom really took up a bunch of your space, huh? What a bitch.' Lucille 2 is confused. 'When she expanded her bathroom and moved your wall over. That's why all your stuff's sticking out.'
jokes.setup: Gob runs into Lucille 2 and opens with apparent commiseration
jokes.punchline: He's helpfully explaining Lucille's theft of Lucille 2's square footage — information Lucille 2 didn't previously have — as a casual icebreaker
jokes.explanation: Gob accidentally sabotages everything Michael just fixed by helpfully explaining the wall theft to Lucille 2 as a friendly aside. His obliviousness to the consequences is perfectly Gob — and 'What a bitch' about his own mother, delivered casually, is a sharp character note.

jokes.text: Lucille 2: 'Your mother really has a way of getting my goat.'
jokes.setup: Gob has just revealed that Lucille literally stole part of her apartment
jokes.punchline: 'Getting my goat' is a dramatically understated response to being told your neighbor physically moved your wall
jokes.explanation: The understatement of 'gets my goat' in response to property theft is a mild but effective joke. Works because the idiom is so quaint relative to the offense.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Oh, and she's not gonna give the shares back now.' Michael: 'You just said that you fixed everything.' Gob: 'Oh, I meant my problems. Yeah, she's gonna make me the new president.'
jokes.setup: Gob announces he 'fixed everything'; Michael assumes he means the company situation
jokes.punchline: 'Oh, I meant my problems.' Gob resolved his own unemployment by getting himself made president — at the cost of everything Michael just accomplished
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best closers. 'Oh, I meant my problems' is a perfect Gob line — the total narcissism, the casual sabotage, the complete absence of self-awareness. The revelation that Gob 'fixed' things by becoming president of a rival-held company is both funny and perfectly consistent with every other joke in his arc this episode. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: Michael: 'You don't need to prove yourself to me, little brother. You will always have a job here. But I do need the keys to the company car.'
jokes.setup: Gob has just catastrophically derailed Michael's plan by getting himself made president of the hostile company
jokes.punchline: Michael can't fire Gob because Gob no longer works for him — so he settles for taking back the car keys as the only leverage he has left
jokes.explanation: The callback to the company car issue — now Michael takes the car back as consolation — closes the episode's car subplot. Michael's powerlessness expressed through petty car-key reclamation is a nice button.

jokes.text: On the next Arrested Development: Lucille 2 'reclaims some space.' [Implied: she moves the wall back or goes further]
jokes.setup: The episode established Lucille's wall theft and Lucille 2 learning about it
jokes.punchline: The teaser implies Lucille 2 will take revenge — 'reclaims some space' as the euphemistic framing mirrors Lucille's original casual framing of the theft
jokes.explanation: A functional 'on the next' callback that mirrors the language of the theft with the language of the revenge. Standard for the show but well-executed.

jokes.text: On the next: Barry after winning the Kings settlement: 'I've wanted a small family restaurant since I got the Kings settlement.' Questioner: 'It still has the dungeon area, right?'
jokes.setup: Barry won his lawsuit against the L.A. Kings and is investing the proceeds
jokes.punchline: He's buying a restaurant — one that has a 'dungeon area' — which suggests the Queen Mary club's dungeon is now a restaurant feature
jokes.explanation: 'The dungeon area' as a restaurant feature implied to be non-negotiable is a good absurdist button. Barry investing his Kings winnings in a former gay club turned family restaurant is a funny rapid convergence of subplots.

tmdb_overview: Michael sells a portion of his stock in the Bluth company to buy a car, and Lucille 2 returns, only to discover that Buster is dating the company secretary Starla.
insight: Seventy-one jokes in 27 minutes—absurdist character work hits peak density for Season 2.
